---
title: "Morse Micro HaLowLink User Guide"
---

# Morse Micro HaLowLink User Guide

## **What is a HaLowLink?**

Morse Micro’s [HaLowLink](https://www.morsemicro.com/halowlink/) is a network infrastructure device which enables [Wi-Fi HaLow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IEEE_802.11ah), a long-range, low-power extension of the wireless networking standard. The HaLowLink is sufficiently flexible and powerful that it can perform all of these key roles:

* A [Wi-Fi HaLow Router and Access Point](#get-started) hosting a new Wi-Fi HaLow network.  
* A [Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point](#get-started) to connect other HaLow-enabled devices to your existing network.  
* A [HaLow Extender](#wi-fi-halow-extender-mode) so that HaLow-less devices, such as computers with 2.4GHz Wi-Fi or Ethernet connections, can benefit from HaLow’s range.

For more information on how these roles can be useful, [please see the **Use Cases** section](#typical-halowlink-use-cases), later in this User Guide.

### **HaLowLink Models**

HaLowLink is available in two forms. Both versions share the same core functionality and run the same HaLowLink implementation of [Morse Micro’s OpenWrt firmware](https://github.com/MorseMicro/openwrt). But while HaLowLink 1 uses the Morse Micro [MM6108 chip](https://www.morsemicro.com/resources/product_brief/MM6108-Product-Brief.pdf), HaLowLink 2 is based on the next-generation [MM8108 part](https://www.morsemicro.com/resources/product_brief/MM8108-Product-Brief.pdf).

Additionally, the HaLowLink 2 is certified for use in a wider range of regulatory territories:

| Product Name | Morse Micro SoC | Regions Supported |
| :---- | :---: | :---- |
| HaLowLink 1 | MM6108 | USA, Canada, Australia |
| HaLowLink 2 | MM8108 | USA, Canada, Australia, UK, Europe, Japan |

The information in this User Guide covers both the HaLowLink 1 and the HaLowLink 2\. It provides a unified set of instructions which apply equally to both models. Where a certain instruction applies only to a specific HaLowLink model, this will be clearly indicated in the text. In all other cases, the term **HaLowLink** (i.e., the product name without a model number suffix) should be taken to refer to all models equally.

### **Security Notice** {#security-notice}

The HaLowLink’s firmware provides access to powerful tools that are used to manage its Wi-Fi HaLow connection and access to any wider networks the unit is connected to. For this reason, the following restrictions have been put in place to help maintain network security:

* **The HaLowLink web interface is accessible only via HTTPS**  
  Any attempt to connect using unencrypted HTTP will automatically redirect to `https://192.168.12.1`. When you first connect, your browser may display a warning that the target page is insecure or the connection is not private. **This is expected**. Though the HaLowLink uses an encrypted HTTPS session, it does so without a signed certificate. You must proceed through these warnings in order to continue accessing the device.  
    
* **SSH is disabled by default** Secure Shell (SSH) access is blocked. To enable it, access the management console and navigate to the **SSH Access** tab on the **Administration** page under **System** in the main HaLowLink menu.

Morse Micro is determined to ensure that its version of the [OpenWrt software](https://github.com/MorseMicro/openwrt) always remains compliant with international cybersecurity standards. To maintain compliance with evolving cybersecurity requirements, and to accommodate the mitigation of new security issues as they emerge, we often update the HaLowLink’s firmware. We strongly recommend that you regularly check for and apply new firmware updates. To learn how the availability of updates is signaled and how you can install updates, please see [**Update the HaLowLink Firmware**](#update-the-halowlink-firmware).

## 

## **A Guided Tour of the HaLowLink**

Before you proceed to set up a new HaLowLink, or reconfigure an existing one, please take a moment to familiarize yourself with its status indicators, controls and connectivity options.

### **LEDs**

The HaLowLink has three LEDs, all mounted on the front of the unit.

#### **![][image1]**

#### **Status** {#status}

| Colour | Pattern | Meaning |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Green | Flashing | Booting into Access Point mode |
| Green | Solid | Operating in Access Point mode |
| Aqua | Flashing | Booting into Extender mode |
| Aqua | Solid | Operating in Extender mode |
| Yellow | Flashing | Factory reset in progress |
| Yellow | Solid | Bootloader running |
| Blue | Flashing | Software update being installed |

| DANGER Do not disconnect the HaLowLink from power when a software update is taking place. |
| :---- |

#### **Wi-Fi HaLow** {#wi-fi-halow}

| Colour | Pattern | Access Point Mode Meaning | Extender Mode Meaning |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| None | None | Wi-Fi HaLow is disabled | Device is not associated via HaLow |
| Purple | Solid | Wi-Fi HaLow is enabled | Device is associated via HaLow |
| Purple | Flickering | Wi-Fi HaLow traffic activity | Wi-Fi HaLow traffic activity |
| Purple | Flashing (slow) | [Pairing](#pairing) in progress | [Pairing](#pairing) in progress |

#### **2.4GHz Wi-Fi 4** {#2.4ghz-wi-fi-4}

| Colour | Pattern | Meaning |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| None | None | Power is off or the 2.4GHz Access Point is inactive. It can be re-enabled via the Web UI or a [factory reset](#restore-factory-settings) |
| Green | Solid | The 2.4GHz Access Point on the HaLowLink is currently active and available for devices to discover and connect to |
| Green | Flickering | 2.4GHz traffic activity |

### **Ports** {#ports}

The HaLowLink’s connectors are all mounted on the back of the unit. Their uses depend on the mode in which the HaLowLink is currently operating.  
![][image2]

#### **Port Roles in Access Point Mode**

| Port | Role |
| :---- | :---- |
| SMA | Connect the supplied Wi-Fi HaLow Antenna |
| RJ-45 WAN | Connect to an existing network/router |
| RJ-45 LAN | Connect a computer to get a `192.168.12.x` IP address and access the management interface at `https://192.168.12.1` |
| USB-C | Power only or power plus Ethernet-over-USB. Connect a computer to get a `192.168.12.x` IP address and access the management interface at `https://192.168.12.1`† |

#### **Port Roles in Extender Mode**

| Port | Role |
| :---- | :---- |
| SMA | Connect the supplied Wi-Fi HaLow Antenna |
| RJ-45 WAN | Connect a computer to use the Extender as a Wi-Fi HaLow adapter |
| RJ-45 LAN | Connect a computer to use the Extender as a Wi-Fi HaLow adapter |
| USB-C | Power only or power plus Ethernet-over-USB. Connect a computer to use the Extender as a Wi-Fi HaLow adapter† |

† If you are not using the bundled power supply, please ensure that the AC adapter or USB port on your computer that you are connecting is capable of delivering full USB voltage.

### **Other Features** {#other-features}

On the side of the HaLowLink you will find the label which lists not only the username and password you will need to use [to log into the unit’s management console](#3.-log-into-the-halowlink-management-console) but also the credentials for its Wi-Fi network when it is hosting one. The Wi-Fi credentials are also embedded in the label’s ready-to-scan QR code.

![][image3]

The HaLowLink’s **Mode** button, which is used to perform a number of operations, including changing the unit from Access Point mode (the default) to Extender mode (see [**Wi-Fi HaLow Extender Mode**](#wi-fi-halow-extender-mode), below) and to [reset the unit to its factory settings](#restore-factory-settings), is located on the unit’s lower narrow side.

## **![][image4]**

## 

## **Get Started** {#get-started}

This section will get you up and running as quickly as possible. It will also introduce you to the HaLowLink’s three operating modes. If you have set up a HaLowLink before, or are looking for guidance on its full range of configuration options, you may prefer to [jump straight to the **Advanced Configuration Options** section](#advanced-configuration-options).

### **1\. Make Your Initial Connections** {#1.-make-your-initial-connections}

Connect the provided antenna to the HaLowLink’s SMA jack first.

Next, connect your computer to the HaLowLink. How you proceed will depend on your preferred connection method:

#### **Ethernet**

1. Power up the HaLowLink using its USB-C port and the provided power supply.  
2. Connect your computer’s Ethernet port to the HaLowLink’s **LAN** port using the provided RJ-45 cable.

#### **Wi-Fi**

1. Power up the HaLowLink using its USB-C port and the provided power supply.  
2. Once the device is fully booted—[the **2.4GHz Wi-Fi** LED](#2.4ghz-wi-fi-4) will be green—connect your computer, tablet or phone to the HaLowLink’s own Wi-Fi network by scanning the QR code or keying in the Wi-Fi SSID and password printed on the HaLowLink’s information label.

#### **USB-C**

Connect your HaLowLink to your computer directly using the USB-C cable provided. This will also power up the HaLowLink.

| WARNING Ensure that the USB port on your computer can provide sufficient power for the HaLowLink (10W). Most modern laptops will have at least one USB-C port that can do so, but if the HaLowLink fails to start, please power it using the bundled adapter and connect to it using one of the methods above. |
| :---- |

### **2\. Optionally, Connect to an Existing Network** {#2.-optionally,-connect-to-an-existing-network}

If you’d like your downstream devices to have access to an existing network through your HaLowLink, connect the HaLowLink’s **WAN** port to an Ethernet port on your router (or any network device with a DHCP server).

### **3\. Log into the HaLowLink Management Console** {#3.-log-into-the-halowlink-management-console}

Once your computer, tablet or phone is connected to the HaLowLink, you can use a web browser to connect to access the unit’s control interface.

1. Enter the web address `https://192.168.12.1` into your browser  
   ![][image5]  
2. Log in using the username and password printed on the [HaLowLink’s information label](#other-features).  
   ![][image6]

| INFO Allow your browser to save your HaLowLink username and password. This will gain you quicker access next time you log in. |
| :---- |

You should now be able to see the console’s **Home** page, which presents a main menu on the left-hand side and, to the right, an initial set of network status cards. To view more information, click on the large number on a card, or on its ↙↗ icon at the top right.

![][image7]

| INFO If you want your Wi-Fi HaLowdevices to have access to your main network or the Internet, make sure the Uplink card does not indicate the HaLowLink is disconnected. |
| :---- |

### **4\. Update the HaLowLink’s Software**

In order to get the latest features, fixes and security upgrades, we recommend checking for a software update as soon as you log in for the first time. This requires an Internet connection, so make sure you have connected the HaLowLink to an Ethernet port on your router as in [Step 2](#2.-optionally,-connect-to-an-existing-network), above.

Click on **Upgrade** in the console’s main menu. Now click the **Check for automatic upgrade** button in the area on the right.

For more details, [please see **Update the HaLowLink Firmware**](#update-the-halowlink-firmware).

### **5\. Run the Setup Wizard** {#5.-run-the-setup-wizard}

When you first power on your HaLowLink, it will be configured as a router with a Wi-Fi HaLowAccess Point. In this case, its [**Status LED**](#status) will be **solid green**. If you’d like to change the unit’s behaviour, click **Wizard** in the HaLowLink’s main menu. The Wizard is the most straightforward way to switch the HaLowLink from one mode to another as it automatically handles all of the many underlying configuration changes.

![][image8]

When you first open the **Wizard** page, confirm that the **Wi-Fi HaLow devices will get an IP on this device’s local network** option is selected—this shows that the device is acting as a router.

| WARNING The Wizard assumes default configuration as a starting point, so it will overwrite customizations you may have made. |
| :---- |

The Wizard lets you select one of three Wi-Fi HaLow modes, and one of three network infrastructure modes.

#### **Wi-Fi HaLow Mode** {#wi-fi-halow-mode}

The Wizard’s **HaLow Mode** options specify how the HaLowLink-hosted Wi-Fi HaLow network operates. By default, the device runs as an Access Point which is sufficient for most use cases thanks to Wi-Fi HaLow’s very long range.

However, if your application requires even greater coverage, or a higher level of redundancy, you can use the Wizard to configure a mesh network built around multiple HaLowLink devices. Two types of mesh are supported: select either **EasyMesh Controller** or **802.11s Mesh**. For details on how these mesh types differ and how to assess which is most suitable for your use case, [please see the Mesh **Network Operation** section](#mesh-network-operation) later in this guide.

| WARNING Mesh setups are considered an advanced option that should not be needed by most users. |
| :---- |

#### **Network Mode** {#network-mode}

The Wizard’s **Network Mode** options define how the HaLowLink will connect to other networks (‘uplinks’), such as your home’s broadband Internet connection or an enterprise switch or router, and the level of network access it provides to downstream Wi-Fi HaLow devices. For additional guidance on which network mode might be best for your application, [please see the **Use Cases** section](#typical-halowlink-use-cases).

##### **Wi-Fi HaLow devices will get an IP on this device’s local network**

In this mode, which is the default, the HaLowLink creates its own local network and DHCP service for Wi-Fi HaLow devices. If the HaLowLink is connected to an upstream router via its **WAN** port, Wi-Fi HaLow devices may reach the Internet, but their IP addresses come from the HaLowLink, not the upstream router. The HaLowLink firewall is active and will block incoming connections on the WAN while forwarding traffic originating from the local Wi-Fi HaLow network out through that same interface.

*Use this for new or standalone Wi-Fi HaLow networks, for isolated test setups, or when you want to specify the IP address range.*

##### **Wi-Fi HaLow devices will get an IP on your existing router’s network**

In this mode, the HaLowLink acts as an Access Point with an uplink via its **WAN** port. Wi-Fi HaLow devices will receive IP addresses from your primary network’s DHCP server, allowing seamless integration with other devices on that network. The HaLowLink’s firewall will be effectively inactive—your existing router is expected to handle this task.

This is the recommended mode. However, it will only work if you have connected the unit’s **WAN** port to your primary network’s router and the HaLowLink has obtained an IP address from it. To confirm this is the case, go to the **Home** page and check that the **Uplink** card shows the HaLowLink’s IP address. If this card shows no IP address, the HaLowLink is not connected to the primary network.

![][image9]

| INFO [Please see the FAQs](#faqs) if you want to ensure your device is fully bridged in this network mode. |
| :---- |

*Use this option when you have a wired Ethernet connection plugged into your HaLowLink‘s WAN port and want Wi-Fi HaLow devices to join your main network directly.*

##### **Wi-Fi HaLow devices will get an IP on this device's local network and use 2.4GHz Wi-Fi for an uplink**

In this mode, the HaLowLink acts as a router with a 2.4GHz Wi-Fi uplink rather than a wired Ethernet uplink. Wi-Fi HaLow devices may reach the Internet, but their IP addresses come from the HaLowLink. The HaLowLink’s firewall is active and will block incoming connections on the uplink while forwarding traffic originating from the local network over 2.4GHz Wi-Fi.

This option requires additional setup after you have selected it: you need to enter your main Wi-Fi network’s credentials. How to do so is shown below.

*Use this option when Ethernet cabling to the external network is unavailable, but you want Wi-Fi HaLow devices to join your main network directly.*

Once you have selected your chosen Wi-Fi HaLow and network modes, click **Save & Apply** to switch to the new setup.

If you are using 2.4GHz Wi-Fi to access your main network, connect to it now:

1. Navigate to the management console’s **Home** page and click **✗ Disconnected** on the **Uplink** card:  
   ![][image10]  
2. Locate your Wi-Fi network in the list of nearby networks that is now displayed and, when requested, enter the network’s password:  
   ![][image11]  
3. Click **Save & Apply**.  
4. Wait for the HaLowLink to connect: you’ll see a **✔** on the **Uplink** card when it has done so.

Congratulations\! Your HaLowLink is now up and running, and delivering Wi-Fi HaLow connectivity to compatible devices.

## 

## **Wi-Fi HaLow Extender Mode** {#wi-fi-halow-extender-mode}

By default, your HaLowLink comes configured as a router with a Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point (AP): the Wi-Fi HaLow network is a separate subnet, but its devices can reach the Internet if the HaLowLink is connected to an Internet-connected network via the unit’s **WAN** port or 2.4GHz Wi-Fi. As shown in the **Get Started** section (see [**Network Modes**](#network-mode), above) it can alternatively operate as a pure AP for a primary network.

These are all AP modes, but the unit can alternatively be used as a HaLowLink Extender—it operates solely as a Wi-Fi HaLow adapter for a connected device. This mode, also known as ‘Station’ (STA) or ‘Client’ mode, allows existing Wi-Fi and Ethernet devices to make use of Wi-Fi HaLow’s long range.

To switch your HaLowLink into Extender mode, follow these steps:

1. Power your HaLowLink via the USB-C port and the provided power supply.  
2. Wait until the [**Status** LED](#status) is **solid green**. If the LED is **solid aqua**, the HaLowLink is already in Extender mode.  
3. Hold down the [**Mode** button](#other-features) on the base of the unit. The **Status** LED will start slowly **flashing green** and then start quickly **flashing aqua**.  
4. Release the button.  
5. Wait until the **Status** LED is **solid aqua**. This indicates it is running in Extender mode.

### **Using the Extender’s Connection**

You can now connect to the unit from another device, either by Ethernet cable, over the unit’s 2.4GHz Wi-Fi link (the credentials are [printed on the unit’s information label](#other-features) and QR code) or via USB-C, though this requires the connected device to provide sufficient power to drive the HaLowLink. The connected device will then be able to operate over the Wi-Fi HaLow network to which the Extender is itself connected.

### **Connect the Extender to Wi-Fi HaLow**

To join a local Wi-Fi HaLow network, the Extender needs to be configured with the network’s credentials. This can be done [manually](#manual-extender-configuration) but we recommend you make use of [Access Point pairing](#pairing) as this is much less error-prone and more resilient to subsequent network credential changes.

#### **Pairing** {#pairing}

Pairing is designed to make connecting your HaLowLink Extender to a HaLowLink Access Point as simple as possible. The Access Point automatically pushes the current settings to paired Extenders as they are added to the network.

For example, if you’ve [used the Wizard](#5.-run-the-setup-wizard) to configure an Access Point as an EasyMesh Controller, every extender you pair will automatically be set up as an EasyMesh Agent. This eliminates the need to manually enter credentials or specify operating modes on each device, reducing errors and ensuring consistent, reliable configuration across your network.

When a HaLowLink is in Extender mode, its management console will not be accessible at the usual `https://192.168.12.1`. Instead, follow these steps to pair it with a HaLowLink Access Point:

1. Press and immediately release [the **Mode** button](#other-features) on the HaLowLink Access Point. Its [**Wi-Fi HaLow** LED](#wi-fi-halow) will begin slowly flashing to indicate the Access Point is ready to pair.  
2. Press and immediately release the **Mode** button on the HaLowLink Extender. Its **Wi-Fi HaLow** LED will begin slowly flashing to indicate it’s searching for an Access Point with which to pair.  
3. Wait until the **Wi-Fi HaLow** LED on the Extender turns **solid purple**. Your Extender has received the settings it needs from the Access Point and applied them. It is ready to use.

If pairing fails, the **Wi-Fi HaLow** LEDs on both devices will flash quickly for 120 seconds, after which the LEDs will stop flashing and you can attempt to pair them again, from Step 1, above. The 120-second delay is a security feature implemented to prevent brute-force access attempts and to limit radio noise.

#### **Manual Extender Configuration** {#manual-extender-configuration}

We strongly recommend that you use [the pairing method](#pairing) whenever possible. If you do need to set the Wi-Fi HaLow SSID and password directly, there is a dedicated interface for this.

1. Check the HaLowLink is in Extender Mode: its [**Status** LED](#status) will be **solid aqua**.  
2. Connect your computer to the Extender using one of the methods described in [Getting Started](#1.-make-your-initial-connections).  
3. Open a web browser on your connected computer and navigate to `https://192.168.12.1`. You’ll see the special **HaLow Extender Configuration** portal:  
   ![][image12]  
4. Enter the details of the Wi-Fi HaLow network you intend to connect to.  
5. Optionally, modify the name of the 2.4GHz Wi-Fi network hosted by the Extender–this is the network to which devices using the Extender will connect.  
6. Click **Save**.

You will now see a page informing you that configuration is complete:

![][image13]

If the credentials are correct, and the network is within range, your Extender should successfully connect to it and its **Wi-Fi HaLow** LED will turn on. If the **Wi-Fi HaLow** LED does not light up as expected, consider performing a [factory reset](#restore-factory-settings) and trying again.

## 

## **Update the HaLowLink Firmware** {#update-the-halowlink-firmware}

| DANGER Do not disconnect the HaLowLink from power when a software update is taking place. The [Status LED](#status) will flash blue while the unit is being updated. |
| :---- |

| INFO The HaLowLink’s firmware can only be updated when it is in Access Point Mode. To update an Extender’s firmware, [factory reset it](#restore-factory-settings) to switch it to Access Point mode and follow the steps below. When you’re done, [switch the unit back to Extender Mode](#wi-fi-halow-extender-mode). |
| :---- |

To update the HaLowLink’s firmware, use your connected laptop, tablet or phone’s web browser to access the unit’s management console at `https://192.168.12.1` as described in [Getting Started](#3.-log-into-the-halowlink-management-console).

Click **Upgrade** in the console’s main menu. Two options will be presented to you:

![][image14]

* Click **Check for automatic upgrade** to attempt to obtain the latest version of the HaLowLink firmware from the Morse Micro servers. This requires an Internet connection, either through the browser you are using to access the HaLowLink GUI or through the HaLowLink device itself via an uplink or otherwise (see [Network Mode](#network-mode)). If an update is available, the console will inform you—click the **Download and Upgrade** button to proceed.

  ![][image15]

* Click **Manually upload firmware file** to upload any compatible firmware that you have previously downloaded to your computer, tablet or phone.

You will now be offered a set of options. Stick with the defaults and click **Continue**.  
![][image16]  
The update will now be installed. **Do not remove power from the HaLowLink** while this is taking place.

| INFO If your HaLowLink device has Internet access, update notifications will appear automatically on the Home page’s Version card. |
| :---- |

## 

## 

## **Restore Factory Settings** {#restore-factory-settings}

A factory reset always returns the HaLowLink to Access Point Mode—its [**Status** LED](#status) is **green**—which is the default. Performing a reset is useful if you:

* Want to switch your HaLowLink back from [Extender Mode](#wi-fi-halow-extender-mode) to [Access Point Mode](#get-started).  
* No longer have access to your device via the network after making configuration changes.  
* Want to start fresh and configure your HaLowLink for a new purpose.

To perform a factory reset:

1. Power on your HaLowLink and wait until the **Status** LED is either **solid green** (Access Point Mode) or **solid aqua** (Extender Mode).  
2. Hold down [the **Mode** button](#other-features) on the base of the unit until the **Status** LED begins slowly **flashing green**.  
3. Release the **Mode** button.  
4. Wait while the **Status** LED is **flashing yellow** (the HaLowLink is resetting) until it changes to **solid green**.  
5. You can now access the unit’s management console at `https://192.168.12.1`, as described in [**Get Started**](#3.-log-into-the-halowlink-management-console).

| WARNING In Step 2, if you continue holding down the button until the Status LED is flashing quickly aqua, the device will reset into [Extender Mode](#wi-fi-halow-extender-mode) rather than Access Point Mode. |
| :---- |

## 

## **Typical HaLowLink Use Cases** {#typical-halowlink-use-cases}

In this section, we’ve compiled some common applications of the HaLowLink. You can use any of them as the basis for your own application, or as scenarios to help you evaluate the product or the technology on which it’s based. We’ve also included some pointers to assist you in setting up the HaLowLink for each use case.

If you plan to use a HaLowLink you have already configured in some way, we suggest that you [perform a factory reset](#restore-factory-settings) before following these instructions, which assume the unit is in its default state.

### **Add a Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point to an Existing Network via Ethernet**

![][image17]  
HaLowLink’s primary use case is to bring Wi-Fi HaLow support to an existing network, allowing any HaLow-enabled client to connect and operate in just the same way as any other Wi-Fi client.

1. Make sure your HaLowLink is in Access Point Mode: its [**Status** LED](#status) is **green**.  
2. Connect the HaLowLink’s [**WAN** port](#ports) to your network. In the home, this usually means placing it next to your router and connecting its **WAN** port to one of the router’s LAN ports.  
3. Wi-Fi HaLow devices can now connect to the Access Point using the SSID and password printed on the HaLowLink’s information label.

Although this approach will work for many use cases, for the best experience we recommend using [the management console’s setup Wizard](#5.-run-the-setup-wizard) to select the **Wi-Fi HaLow devices will get an IP on your existing router’s network** option, which will switch from operating as a router with Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point—i.e., keeping Wi-Fi HaLow devices on their own subnet—to a pure Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point. This ensures Wi-Fi HaLow devices are fully part of your main network.

### **Add a Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point to an Existing Network via 2.4GHz Wi-Fi**

![][image18]  
If it’s not possible to connect your HaLowLink via Ethernet, you can instead connect via 2.4GHz Wi-Fi. You should only do this if you can’t connect by Ethernet as it will require your HaLowLink to act as a router, forwarding traffic from `192.168.12.x` over the Wi-Fi link.

1. Make sure your HaLowLink is in Access Point Mode: its [**Status** LED](#status) is **green**.  
2. Log into the management console at `https://192.168.12.1` and, in the **Wizard**, set the [network mode](#network-mode) to **Wi-Fi HaLow devices will get an IP on this device's local network and use 2.4GHz Wi-Fi for an uplink**. Click **Save & Apply**. Go back to the **Home** page to click on the **Uplink** card and [enter your existing Wi-Fi network’s credentials](#other-features).  
3. Wi-Fi HaLow devices can now connect to the Access Point using the SSID and password printed on the HaLowLink’s information label.

This approach may be particularly useful when working with networks for which you don’t have administrator access, such as corporate WLANs and organizations’ guest networks. In fact, the HaLowLink can act as a travel router in this situation, providing you with your own private network: not just a Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point but also 2.4GHz Wi-Fi access point and Ethernet connectivity.

### **Use Wi-Fi HaLow to Extend an Existing Network** {#use-wi-fi-halow-to-extend-an-existing-network}

![][image19]  
This example requires two HaLowLinks, to establish a Wi-Fi HaLow link between two parts of your network, as if it were an Ethernet cable. First, you should add a HaLowLink Access Point to your network as per either of the two examples above. Once your network supports HaLow, for the second unit you should [follow the instructions in the Extender Mode section](#wi-fi-halow-extender-mode), above. In summary:

1. Make sure the second HaLowLink is in Extender Mode: its [**Status** LED](#status) is **aqua**.  
2. Pair the Extender with the Access Point by pressing and releasing the Access Point’s [**Mode** button](#other-features) then pressing and releasing the **Mode** button on the Extender. The Extender’s [**Wi-Fi HaLow** LED](#wi-fi-halow) will slowly flash, usually for around 10 seconds, before turning solid purple to show it’s connected.  
3. Any device can now connect to the Extender, by USB, Ethernet or 2.4GHz WiFi (use the main network’s SSID and password).

### **Connect Your Computer to a Wi-Fi HaLow Network via USB-C**

![][image20]

1. [Follow the instructions in the previous example](#use-wi-fi-halow-to-extend-an-existing-network) to set up an Extender.  
2. Connect your computer to the Extender using the provided USB-C cable.  
3. Your computer should signal the availability of a new Ethernet adapter. Make sure it’s configured as a DHCP Client.  
4. You can now send traffic via the Wi-Fi HaLow link.

| INFO Because the link appears as an Ethernet connection, your computer will likely use it in preference to any existing wireless connection. See [the Troubleshooting section](#troubleshooting) for more information. |
| :---- |

### **Connect an Ethernet Device to a Wi-Fi HaLow Network**

![][image21]

1. [Follow the instructions above](#use-wi-fi-halow-to-extend-an-existing-network) to set up an Extender.  
2. Connect your device to the Extender using an Ethernet cable.  
3. Your device should now acquire an IP address via DHCP.

### **Connect a Non-Wi-Fi HaLow Wi-Fi Device to a Wi-Fi HaLow Network**

![][image22]  
This could be a computer, tablet, phone or any IoT device.

1. [Follow the instructions above](#use-wi-fi-halow-to-extend-an-existing-network) to set up an Extender.  
2. Get the Extender’s SSID and password (NOT that of the Access Point) by scanning the QR Code on the [Extender’s information label](#other-features) or copying the credentials from the same sticker.  
3. Enter the credentials onto your device.  
4. Your device should now acquire an IP address via DHCP.

### **Experiment with Wi-Fi HaLow**

![][image23]  
If you’re currently experimenting with Wi-Fi HaLow’s amazing range and penetration, the easiest way to do so is with two HaLowLinks and a pair of laptops. The laptops provide power to the HaLowLinks, so you can easily move them around.

1. Connect one laptop to a HaLowLink in default mode (a router with a HaLowLink Access Point) by USB-C.  
2. Log into the management console at `https://192.168.12.1` as described in [Get Started](#3.-log-into-the-halowlink-management-console).  
3. Connect the second laptop to the second HaLowLink, which you should then [switch to Extender Mode as described above](#wi-fi-halow-extender-mode) and [pair to the Access Point](#pairing). You should see the Extender appear on the **Home** page of the Access Point, on the **Connected Devices** card. The **Local Network** card should show the IP addresses of both the Extender and your other laptop.  
   

You can now test out the connection from one laptop to the other. The link is Wi-Fi HaLow. For guidance on how to do this, you can use the [analysis](#status-\>-channel-analysis) and [diagnostic](#network-\>-diagnostics) tools provided by the management console on the Access Point.

| INFO Because the link appears as an Ethernet connection, your computer will likely use it in preference to any existing wireless connection. See [the Troubleshooting section](#troubleshooting) for more information. |
| :---- |

## 

## **Advanced Configuration Options** {#advanced-configuration-options}

The HaLowLink management console has been designed to help you get your unit up and running in your preferred mode of operation as quickly as possible. It also provides full access to the HaLowLink’s underlying [OpenWrt operating software](https://openwrt.org), but for most users’ convenience this functionality, along with the diagnostic and analysis features it offers, isn’t immediately exposed in the UI.

To bring this advanced functionality to the fore, click on **Advanced Config** in the console’s menu. This adds some new entries to the menu. In this section, we’ll focus on those related to customizing the HaLowLink’s setup. We’ll cover monitoring and diagnostic options [in the next section](#explore-wi-fi-halow-connectivity).

### **![][image24]**

| INFO The Advanced Config menu items remain enabled across login sessions. |
| :---- |

### 

### **Save and Apply Settings** {#save-and-apply-settings}

When the **Advanced Config** menu items are hidden, the HaLowLink management console presents a single button, **Save & Apply**, below the configuration options available. If you click this, the changes the console must make to the underlying services are checked, written to a set of configuration files as necessary, and relevant services are restarted. If you click on the button’s disclosure triangle, you can choose one of two alternative save-and-apply options: save and apply the settings as just described, or save and apply without verification. In almost all cases, you will not need to choose the latter option—just clicking on **Save & Apply** will suffice.

If the **Advanced Config** menu items are visible, the HaLowLink management console adds a second button, **Save**, alongside **Save & Apply**. This new button simply records any changes you have made as a series of *pending* changes. This allows you to move through several configuration pages, saving as you go—the console consolidates into a single set the changes you make as you proceed. 

As soon as you begin making changes, this is signaled at the top right of the screen with the appearance of the **Unsaved Changes** indicator followed by the number of underlying changes made so far. This figure may appear greater than you expect, but this is normal: it is the count of changes that will be made to the settings files, not the number of UI elements you have interacted with to instigate those changes.

![][image25]

To view the changes, click the **Unsaved Changes** indicator. Scroll down to close the panel, save and apply all the changes it lists, or clear all the pending changes (the **Revert** button).

![][image26]

If you close the window, perhaps to make further changes, you can subsequently re-open the panel, or just click **Save & Apply** at any location to make the changes.

| INFO For more information about the configuration files and services the console interacts with, and to understand what the listed commands mean, please see the [Command Line Configuration](#file/service-structure) section. |
| :---- |

### **Quick Config** {#quick-config}

For most users, [the **Wizard** page](#5.-run-the-setup-wizard) will be sufficient to configure a HaLowLink Access Point (its [**Status** LED](#status) is **green**). However, if you want to make further adjustments to your configuration, you should use the **Quick Config** page. This provides access to some of the more commonly altered settings enabled by the underlying firmware, such as the Wi-Fi password and encryption method, and whether the HaLowLink has a static IP address or is allocated an address by an external DHCP server.

If the **Advanced Config** menu options are hidden, click **Advanced Config** to reveal the **Quick Config** entry.

| INFO If you need access to settings that Quick Config does not expose, check out [advanced configuration options](#advanced-configuration-options) and [command-line configuration](#command-line-configuration). |
| :---- |

To help you understand the effect of any changes you make, a diagram at the top of the page updates as you alter the settings below.

| WARNING It is easy to change the configuration of your device here in a way that will cause you to lose access to it, particularly if you’re changing network interfaces. If this happens, see [the section on restoring factory settings](#restore-factory-settings). |
| :---- |

The **Quick Config** page is partitioned into sections:

#### **Network Interfaces**

![][image27]  
This section lists the underlying [OpenWrt logical interfaces](https://openwrt.org/docs/guide-user/network/network_configuration#section_interface) available on your HaLowLink, each of which can be configured with either a static IP (and potentially a DHCP server) or as a DHCP client. If you have multiple Ethernet ports or wireless interfaces on the same network, a bridge will automatically be created between them.

#### **Wireless**

![][image28]  
This section will allow you to configure the Wi-Fi interfaces on your HaLowLink, each of which maps to one of the unit’s two radios: Wi-Fi HaLow (`radio1`) and 2.4GHz 802.11n (`radio0`). It is where you attach new wireless interfaces to the network. It is possible to create multiple interfaces for each radio.

| INFO Clicking the gearwheel icons in each of the Quick Config sections’ title bars will take you to the relevant [Advanced Config](#advanced-configuration-options) page where further configuration options are exposed. For more information on the underlying configuration system, known as the Unified Configuration Interface (UCI), please see [Command Line Configuration](#command-line-configuration), below. |
| :---- |

### **Services \> Terminal** {#services->-terminal}

**![][image29]**

Clicking on **Services** in the management console menu exposes a set of additional options. Among them is **Terminal**, which provides easy access to the Linux command line. You will have to re-enter your device password (it’s printed on the label on the bottom of the HaLowLink).

| WARNING The terminal does not work over HTTPS. To use it, follow the browser’s warning link to disable this security feature. |
| :---- |

From the command prompt, you will have access to the standard Linux utilities included in [OpenWrt](https://openwrt.org) and those [presented on the **Diagnostics** page](#network-\>-diagnostics), plus some other useful programs:

| Utility | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- |
| `morse_cli` | Low-level access to information from and settings for the HaLowLink’s Morse Micro MMx108 chip |
| `wavemon` | A terminal-based graphical Wi-Fi signal strength and performance monitor |
| `nano` | A text editor which supports the syntax highlighting of UCI files. You may also use `vi` |
| `tmux` | A terminal multiplexer which enables persistent sessions and windows |

### **System \> Administration** {#system->-administration}

![][image30]

As noted in the [HaLowLink security note](#security-notice) earlier, Secure Shell (SSH) access to the HaLowLink is disabled by default. This is the procedure to enable it:

1. Log into the unit’s management console.  
2. Click **Advanced Config**.  
3. Under **System**, click **Administration**.  
4. Select the **SSH Access** tab.  
5. Check **Enable** under **Dropbear** (*Dropbear* is the HaLowLink’s SSH and SCP (Secure Copy) server).  
6. Adjust any other settings you require for maximum access security.  
7. Click **Save & Apply**.

## 

## **Explore Wi-Fi HaLow Connectivity** {#explore-wi-fi-halow-connectivity}

The HaLowLink comes packed with useful utilities and pages to make the most of your Wi-Fi HaLow connection. In particular, we recommend the following pages, which are only accessible after you have clicked **Advanced Config** in the management console’s main menu.

### **Status \> Channel Analysis** {#status->-channel-analysis}

**![][image31]**

This page will allow you to view the Wi-Fi HaLow channels and the signal strength of any nearby Wi-Fi HaLow networks. If there are a considerable number of nearby Wi-Fi HaLow networks, you may wish to change the channel (using [the **Quick Config** page](#quick-config)) to avoid interference.

### **Status \> Realtime Graphs** {#status->-realtime-graphs}

![][image32]

This page presents a graphical view of the Wi-Fi HaLow link quality as well as other critical system metrics. The view updates continuously while you have the page open.

### **Services \> Range Test**

![][image33]  
The **Range Test** application provides a simple way to analyse Wi-Fi HaLow network performance by automating `iperf3` tests and collecting realtime statistics. It is a useful tool for quickly assessing signal strength, data throughput and connection quality across different environments.

| INFO For the best results, ensure that all devices under test are running the same version of Morse Micro OpenWrt. |
| :---- |

![][image34]  
To use the range testing tool, follow these steps:

1. Connect the devices to be tested to the same Wi-Fi HaLow network. Devices of any type outlined in this guide can be used, but the default HaLowLink Access Point configuration is the most reliable.  
2. Power up the devices in the desired locations, and select one to which to connect your laptop.  
3. Select the IP address of the test device from the **Remote Device** dropdown. If the device you wish to test doesn’t appear on first load, click the 🔍 icon to run another discovery. If this again fails to find the device, you may enter a known IPv4 address into the **Custom** field in the dropdown:  
   ![][image35]  
4. Enter the remote device’s password into the **Password** field. This is printed on the device’s information label. Make sure you enter the correct one—do not use the Wi-Fi password.  
5. Optionally, provide user notes about the test in the **Description** field.  
6. Optionally, enter the location co-ordinates of the local and remote devices, and have the distance between them calculated automatically. Enter decimal degree values. Right-click a location in Google Maps to copy its co-ordinates to your clipboard in the correct format.  
7. Click **Start Test**.

A progress bar will appear showing the status of the current test. The test can be cancelled at any time by clicking the **Stop** button.

After the test completes, its results will be placed in a row in the page’s **Results Summary** sub-section. This result can be deleted by clicking the trash can icon:

![][image36]

A JSON file containing all the raw data, intended to help engineers with remote debugging, can be retrieved by clicking **Download**. The **Download Results Summary (CSV)** button yields a CSV file of all the data represented in the results summary.

| NOTE To avoid overwhelming the HaLowLink’s limited memory resources, test results are not persisted and will be lost if power is removed or the device is rebooted. |
| :---- |

| WARNING If the throughput numbers are returning \~40Mbps for UDP and 900Mbps or more for TCP then the test is likely running over an Ethernet connection. This should be avoided. |
| :---- |

By clicking the gearwheel icon in the top-left corner of the **Test Configuration** sub-section, you can modify the data directions, test length and protocols to be tested.

The remote device will always act as the `iperf3` server and always run the same command:

`iperf3 -s -1 --json`

The local device will always act as the `iperf3` client, running a command in the following format:

`iperf3 -c <ip> <protocol> <direction> -t <time> -O <omit> --json`

* **Protocol** For UDP tests the protocol arguments become `-u -b 40M/30` whereas for TCP tests only `-Z` is set.  
* **Direction** For Send tests no arguments are set. Receive tests set the `-R` flag.  
* **Time** and **Omit** If **Quick Tests** is selected in the **Advanced Configuration** dialog, these arguments will be passed the values `10` and `2`, respectively. By default, the tests will run for longer: `-t 70 -O 10` to allow 10 seconds for rate control to settle and to yield 60 seconds worth of usable data.

### **Network \> Diagnostics** {#network->-diagnostics}

**![][image37]**

This page provides you with a front-end to a number of command line tools to evaluate your network performance and operation, including `iperf3` to test bandwidth, `ping` and `traceroute` to explore connectivity, and `arp-scan` to enumerate all the devices on the network.

| INFO The name of the tool that is being executed is displayed so you can explore the functionality they offer in greater detail by accessing them directly through the command line (see [Terminal](#services-\>-terminal)). |
| :---- |

### **Statistics \> Graphs**

**![][image38]**

Your HaLowLink runs `collectd` to continuously monitor its behaviour, and this page presents that tool’s output in graphical form. Some of the information here is similar to that offered on the [**Realtime Graphs** page](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1jQBapSR_1l3ULlURL59KFX0zqRPq8oQyEkiIy_JQqyk/edit#realtime-graphs), but it’s updated at a lower frequency and continues to be gathered as long as the device is running, rather than just while you’re on the page, as is the case with **Realtime Graphs**. It is also possible to configure other devices, such as HaLowLink Extenders to deliver log data to this page, allowing you to aggregate all your statistics in a single place.

## 

## **Mesh Network Operation** {#mesh-network-operation}

| WARNING This feature is not currently supported in the EU or UK. Those locations’ regulatory environments mandate dynamic channel changing during operation. This is currently implemented on a per-device basis, but mesh devices in the same mesh must all have the same channel. |
| :---- |

Mesh technology can extend network coverage and improve redundancy. It does so by allowing multiple devices, called mesh ‘nodes’, to co-ordinate so they can route traffic more directly from one to another or via others, rather than rely on a router as the sole intermediary between nodes. This can improve the quality of large-scale networks, but for small installations, where nodes are no closer to each other than they are to the router, there is little benefit to be gained—and often performance is poorer because of the additional network traffic overhead that mesh adds.

![][image39]  
*Alternative network topologies: the traditional network star topology (left) and a web-like mesh (right)* 

### **Should I Use a Mesh?**

Mesh networks allow the use of multiple HaLowLink devices to extend network coverage. However, such setups are complex, often misunderstood, and can even degrade network performance if deployed improperly. For most use cases, a single HaLowLink Access Point should provide more than enough coverage.

If you are considering whether to implement a mesh, you should bear in mind the following limitations of mesh networks:

* Each additional hop reduces the throughput by half.  
* The number of hops is limited.  
* There is a considerable overhead of beacon and control traffic.

You should consider implementing a mesh if:

* You have dead zones or unreachable areas.  
* You need a reliable and redundant network with multiple paths more than you need data transfer speed.

You likely **do not need a mesh** if:

* Your existing Access Point and Extender Wi-Fi HaLow network already provides sufficient coverage.  
* You only have two HaLowLink devices.

### **Establish a Mesh**

To set up a mesh, configure your HaLowLink Access Point (the [**Status** LED](#status) is **green**) as either an EasyMesh Controller or an 802.11s Mesh peer. Make your choice by applying the relevant [**HaLow Mode** option on the **Wizard** page](#wi-fi-halow-mode) of the management console.

When you [pair additional HaLowLink Extenders](#pairing) they will automatically receive the corresponding 802.11s Mesh Point or EasyMesh Agent configurations from the host Access Point.

| WARNING If you have configured your device in one of the mesh modes, avoid making significant networking changes in the management console as it is easy to misconfigure your setup. For EasyMesh specifically, some settings are managed automatically and are not made visible in the management console. |
| :---- |

### **HaLowLink Mesh Type Comparison**

The HaLowLink supports two alternative foundations for mesh networks, [EasyMesh](https://www.wi-fi.org/alternative-topologies) and [802.11s Mesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IEEE_802.11s). Both complement each other: which you select will largely depend upon the extent of your planned installation—or the installation into which your product will become a part. The table below summarizes the key features and roles of the two standards.

| Feature | EasyMesh | 802.11s Mesh |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Best For | Smaller fixed networks which require greater coverage for end devices | Larger or complex topologies requiring advanced routing and redundancy |
| Example Use Case | Expanding coverage within a single building or site | Multi-building, industrial, or outdoor deployments needing fault tolerance |
| Controller & Topology | Controller-managed tree | Distributed peer-to-peer |
| Redundancy | Partial support with STA steering | Designed for multiple paths |
| Node Mobility | Designed for fixed devices only | Supported |
| Standards | Wi-Fi Alliance EasyMesh Standard | IEEE 802.11s |
| Co-located Wi-Fi HaLow Access Points | Wi-Fi HaLow STAs can connect to any AP node in network | 802.11s Mesh Gates can be manually configured with an AP |
| Maximum Peer Nodes | 4 | 10 |
| Maximum Hops | 2 | 4 |
| Performance | Lower latency single backhaul per tree | Slower due to higher overhead on multiple peer links |

#### **EasyMesh**

![][image40]  
EasyMesh is a Wi-Fi Alliance specification for combining Access Points in a tree structure to extend coverage (but with reduced bandwidth available to clients). HaLowLink EasyMesh Controllers discover, configure and monitor EasyMesh Agents, ensuring the use of consistent SSIDs and channel information across the mesh.

Agents connect upstream towards the Controller as normal clients while simultaneously serving downstream clients as Access Points, allowing any Wi-Fi HaLow device with the correct credentials to connect. Agents can also onboard client devices onto the mesh via Ethernet (any method) and the built-in 2.4GHz AP.

The Access Point management console provides an EasyMesh visualizer: click on the **EasyMesh Controller** card on the **Home** page. This will display the connected nodes in a tree structure:

![][image41]

#### **802.11s Mesh**

| DANGER 802.11s Mesh support is currently a beta feature and so intended for experimental use only. It is not intended for production use at this time. |
| :---- |

![][image42]

802.11s Mesh is an IEEE Wi-Fi mesh standard which forms a truly distributed peer-to-peer multi-hop Mesh Basic Service Set (MBSS). Configured under **HaLow Mode** on the **Wizard** page, 802.11s Mesh networks aim to increase coverage, redundancy and range by establishing self-healing links between neighbouring nodes. Only mesh-capable devices can join or make use of the functionality provided by the MBSS.

802.11s Mesh HaLowLink networks can be accessed via any Ethernet port or 2.4GHz Wi-Fi. Units in Access Point Mode also run a co-located Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point which can allow the mesh to be bridged to external Wi-Fi HaLow networks.

The Access Point management console provides an 802.11s Mesh visualizer. Click on the **802.11s Mesh Topology** card on the **Home** page of any HaLowLink configured to operate in an 802.11s Mesh—it will display the topology as seen by that node:

![][image43]

### **Pairing Extenders with a Mesh**

As mentioned above, if you pair a HaLowLink Extender with a Mesh-enabled HaLowLink Access Point, the Extender will automatically become part of the mesh, either an 802.11s Mesh Point or an EasyMesh Agent.

If you prefer that the Extender remains a normal station/client device instead of a mesh peer, then you should [configure the Extender manually](#manual-extender-configuration). A manually configured device will not automatically become part of the mesh.

In short, if you:

* Pair the Extender via [the **Mode** button](#other-features), it will become part of the mesh.  
* Manually configure the Extender via the web UI, it will remain a normal client.

#### **EasyMesh**

Once a device is configured as an Agent, the role of the **Mode** button changes: it will onboard new devices to the mesh. This means that you can easily add more devices to the mesh from any of the already configured devices. If you want to reconfigure your Extender entirely, you should [perform a factory reset](#restore-factory-settings) before re-pairing.

The SSID of the original HaLowLink Access Point is propagated to all devices in the mesh both for the 2.4GHz WiFi and Wi-Fi HaLow. This means the QR codes on your Extenders can no longer work to connect, but you will have a consistent SSID across the mesh.

#### **802.11s Mesh**

Unlike EasyMesh, any Extender will **not** have an additional Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point, as the Wi-Fi HaLow interface will be exclusively used for the mesh. The 2.4GHz Access Point on the Extender is unmodified, so that all Extenders will have a distinct SSID and password (by default the one in the QR code). In this mode, Extenders will not support any further pairing actions, and so must be [factory reset](#restore-factory-settings) to be re-paired.

If you want to connect Wi-Fi HaLow client devices that do not support 802.11s Mesh to 802.11s Mesh Extenders, you can manually add a Wi-Fi HaLow Access Point via the **Quick Config** section of the Web UI. This will also re-enable the pairing function of the **Mode** button, allowing you (as with EasyMesh) to onboard additional Extenders with this Extender. You will still need to [perform a factory reset](#restore-factory-settings) if you want to re-pair the existing Extender.

## 

## **Command Line Configuration** {#command-line-configuration}

The software powering the HaLowLink is based on [OpenWrt](https://openwrt.org), an open Linux-based operating system for network-connected devices. OpenWrt maintains a configuration mechanism called [the Unified Configuration Interface (UCI)](http://openwrt.org/docs/guide-user/base-system/uci), which is essentially a defined set of config files composed in a specific format and stored in the unit’s `/etc/config` directory.

The HaLowLink management console provides three tiers of mediated access to the unit’s settings. [**Advanced Config**](#advanced-configuration-options) provides a graphical front-end to most of the settings; [**Quick Config**](#quick-config) does the same but over a much narrower range of settings. The [**Wizard**](#5.-run-the-setup-wizard) essentially switches between a small number of alternative configurations.

We recommend using these graphical mechanisms in reverse order—Wizard first, then Quick Config, then Advanced Config—as you reach down for the level of settings access you need. If none of these suffice, you have one further level: direct access to the UCI via the command line.

You can directly access the OpenWrt config files via either SSH ([which must be enabled first](#system-\>-administration)) or the management console’s **Terminal** page.

### **Make Changes**

This is a two-step process:

#### **1\. Set new values in the UCI.**

You can make changes to UCI via the `uci` command line tool, or by editing the files in `/etc/config`. For example:

`nano /etc/config/wireless`

or

`uci show wireless`  
`uci set wireless.default_radio0.mode=sta`  
`uci commit`

Running `uci commit` will cause the changes to be written to `/etc/config/wireless`.

#### **2\. Reload the configuration to apply the changes.**

Once you've updated the configuration files, either by editing them directly or through the `uci` command, run `reload_config` to make the changes take effect. It checks to see if any of the config files have changed and, if they have, reloads any services affected by these changes.

There are other ways to do this:

* Explicitly reload a single service: `service <service> reload`.  
* Explicitly restart a single service: `service <service> restart`.  
* Bring down and then bring back up only the Wi-Fi interfaces without restarting the network: `wifi down && wifi up`.

| INFO Manually triggering a reload will pick up uncommitted changes. |
| :---- |

### **Understand GUI Changes**

If you’ve clicked **Advanced Config** in the main menu, the management console allows you to pend changes as you make them rather than apply them immediately. Pending changes are recorded as a sequence of `uci set` commands like those described above, which you can subsequently view and then apply or reject.

To learn how to save changes to the list of pending changes, view that list, and apply or revert the changes, please see [**Save and Apply Settings**](#save-and-apply-settings).

### **File/Service Structure** {#file/service-structure}

The file `/etc/config/wireless` lists the radio devices (`wifi-device`) and the interfaces connected to each of them (e.g., `wlan0`, `wlan1`). Any `wifi-iface` has a `network` value, which refers to an interface in the `network` UCI file. This file corresponds to the **Wireless** section on the **Quick Config** page.

The file `/etc/config/network` contains a mix of switches/bridges and [logical interfaces](https://openwrt.org/docs/guide-user/network/network_configuration#section_interface). An `interface` in a network may point to a bridge, in which case multiple Ethernet ports or `wifi-iface`s might be attached to it. Confusingly, the wireless interfaces are not directly mentioned here, only in the `wireless` file. This means it’s possible to incorrectly configure a network interface by not having a bridge and having multiple `wifi-iface`s refer to it. This file corresponds to the **Network Interfaces** section on the **Quick Config** page.

The file `/etc/config/firewall` controls `nftables`—i.e., forwarding/masquerading as well as simple accept/reject. Firewalls have another level of indirection, called ‘zones’, such that you can potentially place multiple network interfaces in one zone. `dhcp` controls `dnsmasq` (i.e., DHCP and DNS). Usually, `dnsmasq` is always running, but if you don’t want DHCP active on particular interfaces, you set them to `ignore`.

### **Debugging**

If you’ve made a change and it’s not working the way you expect, this command is useful for following along with the log as new entries are generated:

`logread -l 100 -f`

This is OpenWrt’s primary mechanism for reporting that something went wrong, since you won’t see it running `reload_config`.

| INFO If you’ve manually edited the configuration files rather than called `uci set`, it’s possible you have made them invalid. Use `uci show` to confirm that the UCI library can still parse the files. |
| :---- |

## 

## **FAQs** {#faqs}

**How can I make my HaLowLink Access Point behave exactly like a standard, fully bridged access point?**  
If you have [used the Wizard](#5.-run-the-setup-wizard) to change your Network Mode to the recommended **Wi-Fi HaLow devices will get an IP on your existing router's network** setting, you will have effectively set up a normal bridged Access Point—except for a management network, which hosts the console at `https://192.168.12.1`. This management network is maintained for ease of reconfiguration, but can be removed.

If you want your HaLowLink Access Point to behave exactly like a consumer Access Point product (i.e., it is fully bridged), go to the **Quick Config** page and move all the devices and ports (LAN/2.4GHz/USB\_C) from the LAN network onto the WLAN network using the **Network Interfaces** section. This will allow you to connect devices to your normal network via the LAN port, the 2.4GHz Wi-Fi AP and even the USB-C port but it will remove access to the separate management subnet.

Ideally, you should do this from the address assigned from your upstream network (i.e. not `192.168.12.1`) to avoid losing access to the device. If you do use `192.168.12.1`, you should use the **Apply Unchecked** option, as otherwise the changes will automatically revert unless you very quickly re-access the console via the other address in the same browser.

**How can I test mesh networking?**  
There are two recommended ways to observe mesh operation, each using three HaLowLink devices:

1. **Confirm relaying through an intermediate node** Move Devices 1 and 2 far enough apart that they can no longer communicate directly before introducing Device 3 as a mesh peer. Successful communication between Devices 1 and 2 in this state indicates that traffic is being relayed via Device 3\.  
2. **Demonstrate throughput improvement** Setup Devices 1 and 2 as above but increase the distance between them until their throughput decreases significantly. Now introduce Device 3 as a mesh peer. An improvement in throughput confirms that the mesh path has been established.

**How can I test 802.11s Mesh Self-Healing when my HaLowLink nodes are too close together?**  
If you are unable to separate your HaLowLink 802.11s mesh devices sufficiently to observe the self-healing behaviour, do not be surprised—this is expected behaviour\! Wi-Fi HaLow’s primary strength is its range.

To simulate greater distance between your HaLowLink 802.11s Mesh peers for testing purposes, you can adjust the RSSI threshold for joining. This prevents nodes from forming mesh links when the received signal strength is too weak.

1. Set up your 802.11s Mesh.  
2. In the **Advanced Config** menu, navigate to **Network** and then **Wireless**.  
3. Click **Edit** on the **Mesh Point**:  
   ![][image44]  
4. Scroll down to **Interface Configuration** and click the **Mesh Settings** tab.  
5. Update the value in the **RSSI threshold for joining** field to define an appropriate minimum signal strength a node must detect to establish a peer link in the mesh:  
   ![][image45]  
6. Click **Save** on the dialog and then **Save & Apply** on the **Wireless** page to apply these changes.

**How can I limit or increase the number of peers allowed in my 802.11s Mesh?**  
Follow the same steps listed in the previous question up to Step 3). From there you can adjust the maximum number of mesh peers. If you set this value to `2`, it will force the mesh to form a linear chain topology, which is likely not useful.

**I changed the mode of an interface on the Quick Config page, and it's refusing to save or not working as expected. What should I do?**

| INFO Before attempting any significant interface modifications on [the Quick Config page](#quick-config), you should confirm that none of the Wizard configurations meet your needs. |
| :---- |

This usually occurs when you are trying to attach a non-WDS (four-address mode) interface to a WDS (Wireless Distribution System) bridge, such as adding a 2.4GHz Wi-Fi client to a HaLowLink Access Point. For technical reasons, only one non-WDS interface (three-address mode) can exist on a bridge interface at a time. Any attempt to change this will cause errors to pop up on [the **Quick Config** page](#quick-config) when saving changes.

To provide more context to this error, we can expand upon the recommended actions described in the example error message:

1. **Remove the non-WDS Wi-Fi client** The error message describes the offending non-WDS Wi-Fi client, which can be removed. In the example error message pictured above it specifies that the client on the WAN network with the SSID `YourWifiNetwork` is causing problems. If this was just added or modified, reset the changes using the **Reset** button in the bottom right corner of the page:  
   ![][image46]  
   If the non-WDS client already existed before making the changes which caused the error message, you can simply delete the non-WDS client using the trashcan button (not recommended) or move it to a new network where it is the only device (recommended). To create a new network interface, go to the **Network Interfaces** subsection on the same page and enter a new network interface name before clicking the **Add** button:  
   ![][image47]  
   You should also click **Add Zone** to create a new permissive firewall zone. The created network interface will be an empty WDS bridge which will allow the attachment of a singular non-WDS Wi-Fi client interface. You can now safely move the offending non-WDS Wi-Fi client to this new interface without deleting it. For example, see the non-WDS `YourWifiNetwork` 2.4GHz Wi-Fi client moved to the newly created `24lan` network interface below:  
   ![][image48]  
2. **Enable WDS for the Wi-Fi clients (if possible)** From the **Wireless** subsection you should be able to select **Client (WDS)** as a mode for your Wi-Fi HaLow Wi-Fi client devices only.  
3. **Remove all other devices from this network to leave a single non-WDS Wi-Fi client** This option is simply the inverse of **Remove the non-WDS Wi-Fi client** and involves:  
   * De-selecting the Ethernet ports from the relevant dropdown in the **Network Interfaces** subsection. You may also want to select them on another network interface row to keep them in use.  
   * Moving the wireless devices onto other network interfaces via the **Wireless** subsection.

You can also delete them altogether if you prefer.

## 

## **Troubleshooting** {#troubleshooting}

If you’re having trouble with your HaLowLink, we recommend first [performing a factory reset](#restore-factory-settings). You can also [reach out on the Morse Micro Community Forum](https://community.morsemicro.com/) to share your issue and get help from our experts and other users. The table below lists some of the more common issues our users have encountered and their solutions.

| Problem | Solution |
| :---- | :---- |
| The [**Status** LED](#status) is not illuminated | There is a problem with power to the device. Check that the USB-C port on the HaLowLink is connected to the provided power supply or to a USB-C port on a computer. Do not use a USB-A to USB-C adapter |
| The [**Status** LED](#status) is still yellow after some time, or never stops flashing green after boot | The flash partition is probably corrupt. To recover your HaLowLink, please see [**Recovering from failed updates**](#recovering-from-failed-updates), below |
| I can’t access the HaLowLink at `https://192.168.12.1` | If the status light is solid green: Make sure your computer is connected to either the LAN or USB port of the HaLowLink. ● Check that your network connection is configured as a DHCP client, and has been allocated a `192.168.12.x` IP. ● If you’re failing to establish a network connection to your device, see [**Restore Factory Settings**](#restore-factory-settings), above. If the **Status** LED is solid aqua, your HaLowLink is in Extender mode and will only be useful if connected to an Access Point. Simply connect other devices to it via Ethernet or 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi to use your HaLow-enabled network. You should not need to access its Web UI, but you may find its address in the DHCP lease table of your DHCP server |
| I changed a configuration setting and now I can’t access my HaLowLink, but I don’t want to reset it | If you need to access your HaLowLink as part of troubleshooting a complex configuration change, you can connect your computer to the LAN or WAN port and configure it with the following settings: IP `10.22.121.110`, netmask `255.255.255.254` (if you can’t set `255.255.255.254` due to OS limitations, use `255.255.255.0`). As long as your HaLowLink has a solid green or aqua light, it will be available at `10.22.121.111` (this is a secondary static IP assigned for diagnostic purposes) |
| I can see that the **Access Point** card has connected devices, but the **Local Network** card doesn’t list them | If you have configured your device as an Access Point only (i.e., the Wizard option **Wi-Fi HaLow devices will get an IP on your existing router’s network**), then these devices will appear in the DHCP lease table of the existing DHCP server on your network. The Local Network on the HaLowLink is being used primarily for easy access to the configuration, and will not have Wi-Fi HaLow devices in it. Alternatively, if you have temporarily lost power to HaLowLink Access Point your devices may not yet have refreshed their DHCP leases. They will eventually renew their leases when their lease time expires |
| When I connect my computer to my HaLowLink Extender over Ethernet, Internet access slows down | Because your computer has a wired connection to the HaLowLink, most operating systems will prefer this connection over any wireless connection. However, if you’re using a HaLowLink Extender, this means you will be restricted to the maximum bandwidth over the Wi-Fi HaLow link. For your particular operating system, you should determine how to prefer your existing Wi-Fi connection (e.g., via setting the HaLowLink connection to local only, removing the default route from the HaLowLink, or changing the route priorities) |
| When I connect my computer to my HaLowLink directly over Ethernet, I lose Internet access | This is the same problem as above, where your computer will prefer the HaLowLink connection over any wireless connection, but in this case it’s probably because your HaLowLink isn’t yet connected to the internet. For an Access Point, check the **Uplink** card on the **Home** page (which will report the WAN and 2.4GHz state). For an Extender check it has a purple light *and* that the Access Point is correctly configured |
| I changed the [HaLow mode](#wi-fi-halow-mode) or [network mode](#network-mode) of my HaLowLink, and my Extenders no longer work | If you make significant changes to your Access Point configuration, any attached Extenders might not continue to function as you expect. If you’ve just changed the [network mode](#network-mode), we recommend power cycling your Extender to force it to reinitialize. If you’ve changed the Wi-Fi HaLow mode, you should [follow the instructions in the Extender section](#pairing) to reset your device to Extender mode and re-perform the pairing procedure |
| My connection isn’t performing as I expect | Check the signal strengths on the Access Point by going to the **Home** page and clicking on the **Connected Devices** card. For more detailed information about the signal you can [navigate to the **Realtime Graphs** page](#status-\>-realtime-graphs). To check if there are other Wi-Fi HaLow networks interfering with yours, you can [check the **Channel Analysis** page](#status-\>-channel-analysis). To run `iperf3` or `ping` tests, you can also [use the **Diagnostics** page](#network-\>-diagnostics) |
| My Extender’s [**Wi-Fi HaLow** LED](#wi-fi-halow) is flashing quickly all the time even when I’m not using it. | The wireless and wired connection are bridged on the Extender, so make sure you haven’t created a network loop by connecting a LAN/WAN port to the same network as your Access Point. To solve this, disconnect the incorrect Ethernet link |
| I’m seeing strange or confusing behavior not mentioned above | Make sure you have the latest firmware by going to [the **Upgrade** page](#update-the-halowlink-firmware) and then clicking **Check for automatic upgrade**. If you would like to report an issue to Morse Micro, go to the **Support** page under **Help**, then click **Create Archive**. You can then post this on [the Morse Micro Community Forum](https://community.morsemicro.com/) and let us know what the problem was |

### **Recovering From Failed Updates** {#recovering-from-failed-updates}

If for some reason the HaLowLink’s firmware has become corrupted or is not booting—this is most often caused by loss of power during an update—the following procedure will allow a new image to be written to flash.

Before proceeding, download the latest firmware.

1. Remove all cables from the device.  
2. Connect your computer to the HaLowLink directly by Ethernet cable.  
3. While the HaLowLink is powered off, press and hold [its **Mode** button](#other-features).  
4. Attach the power cable, turn on power and watch for the **Status** LED first to turn yellow and then blink white five times.  
5. Release the **Mode** button. The LED should go yellow.  
6. Set your computer’s Ethernet connection to the static IP address `192.168.12.2` and the netmask `255.255.255.0`  
7. Open a web browser and navigate to `http://192.168.12.1`. 

| WARNING If you get a `Page Not Found` error message during this step, ensure that the URL entered into the browser only contains the IP address and no path. You may also need to clear your browser’s cache and/or operate it in private/incognito mode. |
| :---- |

8. Use the UI to upload a firmware file and then click **Update Firmware**. A progress screen will appear to monitor the update:  
   ![][image49]

| INFO You can safely navigate away from this page. |
| :---- |

9. Do not remove the power until the device has installed the firmware and fully rebooted. This [**Status** LED](#status) sequence will indicate progress:  
   1. Solid purple.  
   2. Red/purple flashing.  
   3. Solid purple.  
   4. Solid red.  
   5. Off (rebooting).  
   6. Flashing yellow.  
   7. Solid yellow (the start of the normal boot process).

| WARNING The entire process can take more than ten minutes to complete. |
| :---- |
